topic: Telluric ~12 Hz Literature Peak — change-detected re-review (post-hoc paragraph added)
change_summary: detail_verdict_length grew from 151 (c2) to 1114 (current) — +638% drift on a single field; pre-tldr-schema fingerprint, compared on common fields only; sourced from drift-audit.json generated 2026-05-05T20:52Z. Drift driven by the systematic post-hoc-framework rollout that appended a standardized paragraph anchoring each post_hoc=true WIN to Part 3.1b. Verdict (Refuted by Data) and detail_evidence (592 chars) unchanged from c2.
our_argument_summary: Our review says the dome’s claimed ~12 Hz ‘telluric peak’ is the inter-harmonic spectral minimum between the Schumann fundamental (~7.83 Hz) and first harmonic (~14.3 Hz) — i.e., a trough predicted by spherical-cavity physics, not a feature of disc geometry. detail_evidence cites Chave & Jones (2012) via DOI 10.1017/CBO9781139020138 and cross-references WIN-008 for the full analysis. The new c3-era post-hoc paragraph in detail_verdict_text adds a Part 3.1b post-hoc tag rationale: the confirming value comes from public, indexed MT/telluric records the author plausibly knew at write-time, with no independent pre-registration; legitimate-novelty carve-out fails.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Dome’s WIN list states only ‘Multiple independent MT literature sources. Telluric ~12 Hz peak consistent across independent datasets.’ No formula links 12 Hz to dome geometry; no derivation chain from disc_radius, firmament_height, or any aetheric parameter is published. The claim is a citation to literature, not a calculation.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Display only — the dome’s on-site numeric for this WIN is a stated value with no internal computation visible in the wins.txt or predictions.txt extracts in raw-text/. monitor.py is not present in raw-text/ for this clone (raw-text contains only the rendered site dumps), so we rely on prior reviews and the dome’s own framing for the hardcoded tag. Cycle 1/2 reviewers asserted hardcoded with no live fetch; nothing in the current site text contradicts that.
data_sources_used: unspecified MT literature (no DOIs cited by the dome itself)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The dome’s phrasing ‘consistent across independent datasets’ is unfalsifiable as written — no specific dataset, frequency tolerance, or spectral threshold is given. A defender can always claim some particular MT site shows elevated power at 12 Hz; the claim cannot be tested without a quantitative spec.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: raw-text/02-wins.txt records WIN-009 as a literature-citation claim with no API/URL fetch behind it. Cycle 1 and Cycle 2 reviewers both classified this WIN’s monitoring as hardcoded. monitor.py source is not in this clone’s raw-text/ snapshot (raw-text/ contains only the rendered site text dumps), so we cannot independently re-verify against current monitor.py source. Tag retained from prior cycles; flag this as a methodology footnote, not a fresh independent verification.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Schumann inter-harmonic power minima are a standard prediction of spherical Earth-ionosphere cavity physics (Chave & Jones, 2012; Sentman, 1995). The dome relabels a region known to be a trough as a ‘peak’ of ‘telluric resonance’ — the rename is the contribution; the underlying spectrum is the textbook Schumann band.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Schumann fundamental was first directly measured by Balser & Wagner (1960); harmonics including 14.3 Hz have been catalogued for decades. Any ‘peak’ at 12 Hz claimed in MT literature is observable and indexed long before the dome model. Under the framework in Part 3.1b, neither the temporal-impossibility carve-out nor the provable-unawareness carve-out applies.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No formula on the dome site derives 12 Hz from disc_radius (20,015 km), firmament_height (9,086 km), or any other dome parameter. The dome’s flat-disc cavity-mode formulas (e.g., f = c/(2D), f = v_a/(2 r_disc)) yield 11.79 Hz only when D is silently set to Earth’s diameter — a globe parameter — and produce 7.49 Hz when r_disc is used directly. 12 Hz is not produced by any single dome derivation.

holes_found.description: The post-hoc paragraph references ‘the data-fetch line’ in the monitor script: ‘the monitor script hardcodes the target number on the same commit as the data-fetch line, so the temporal ordering the definition requires cannot be checked from the repository history.’ For monitoring=hardcoded WINs (which this is), there may be no data-fetch line at all — just a hardcoded predicted=12.0 / observed=12.0 pair. The current phrasing implies a fetch exists alongside the hardcoded value. A dome defender will read this and say: ‘Our monitor doesn’t fetch anything for WIN-009, so your post-hoc accusation is misaimed.’ The substantive post-hoc charge still lands (the value is a textbook number plausibly known to the author), but the specific commit-history mechanism cited doesn’t fit a no-fetch WIN.
holes_found.recommendation: Tighten the boilerplate so it works for both hardcoded-with-fetch and hardcoded-without-fetch WINs. Suggested replacement: ‘the monitor script hardcodes both the predicted and the observed values — there is no independent data-fetch step, so the temporal ordering the definition requires cannot be checked at all.’ This is a templated paragraph used across many WINs; consider proposing the fix at the framework level rather than per-WIN.

holes_found.description: Latent strawman risk on the telluric-vs-Schumann distinction. detail_evidence describes the spectrum in atmospheric-Schumann terms (‘7.83 Hz fundamental, ~14.3 Hz first harmonic’), but the dome’s WIN-009 explicitly labels its claim ‘telluric’ (subsurface electrical currents) and ‘Literature Peak’ (MT survey datasets). A fair-minded defender will argue we’re refuting a different phenomenon: ‘Schumann is atmospheric ELF; we’re claiming a telluric (ground-current) peak — separate measurement, separate spectrum, separate cavity.’ The accurate counter is that magnetotelluric measurements at ELF frequencies are dominated by Schumann-band signals propagating into the ground, so the telluric MT spectrum and the atmospheric Schumann spectrum are the same modes seen via different sensors. Our text doesn’t make that bridge explicit, leaving the defender an open lane.
holes_found.recommendation: Add one sentence to detail_evidence after the Chave & Jones citation: ‘MT (magnetotelluric) signals at ELF frequencies are dominated by the same Earth–ionosphere cavity excitations measured atmospherically as Schumann modes; the underground spectrum is the surface spectrum coupled into the ground, not an independent resonance.’ This preempts the ‘you’re comparing apples to oranges’ escape.

holes_found.description: Generic post-hoc source list mismatch. The boilerplate paragraph lists ‘(IGRF, NASA station archives, ocean-tide gauges, telluric records, etc.)’ as example public datasets. For WIN-009 only ‘telluric records’ (and arguably MT/ELF spectral databases, which aren’t in the list) are relevant. IGRF and ocean-tide gauges have nothing to do with this WIN. Compare WIN-008’s post-hoc paragraph, which says ‘published MT and ELF spectral literature’ — cleaner, on-target. The mismatch is harmless because the list ends in ‘etc.’ but it dilutes the rigor signal and reads like template boilerplate rather than per-WIN reasoning.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the source list in WIN-009’s post-hoc paragraph with WIN-008’s phrasing (‘published MT and ELF spectral literature’). Or, if the framework prefers a uniform list, drop the parenthetical entirely and just say ‘a public, indexed dataset.’ Low priority; cosmetic.

holes_found.description: detail_evidence for WIN-009 is currently brief (592 chars) and outsources the heavy lifting to WIN-008 (‘See WIN-008 for full analysis’). That is a defensible factoring — the two WINs share the same physics — but it makes WIN-009’s standalone reading thin: a reader who jumps to WIN-009 from the predictions table sees a one-paragraph rebuttal and has to follow a link to learn why the harmonic at 14.3 Hz disproves the cutoff claim. The summary table ‘finding’ already carries the punch; the standalone detail could absorb one more sentence so the cross-link is supplemental rather than load-bearing.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a single quantitative line to detail_evidence summarizing the harmonic spectrum (‘measured Schumann harmonics: 7.83, 14.3, 20.8, 27.3, 33.8 Hz — the existence of all four above 11.7 Hz directly falsifies any cutoff or peak in the inter-harmonic gap’). This is data, not duplication, and it lets WIN-009 stand on its own.

stronger_arguments.description: The dome’s own monitor for the Schumann domain (per WIN-002 c2/c3 review) hardcodes predicted=7.83, observed=7.83. If WIN-002’s monitor knows the Schumann fundamental is 7.83 Hz, then WIN-009’s claim of a separate ‘peak at ~12 Hz’ lives in a spectrum the same author has already measured — yet the dome publishes no MT spectrum showing the 12 Hz feature, only a literature citation. Our review could note that the dome’s own monitoring infrastructure measures the Schumann band but conspicuously fails to verify the 12 Hz peak it claims, despite measuring on the same hardware.

Chave & Jones (2012) is the canonical reference but a reader-friendly addition would be a recent open-access spectrum (e.g., the GFZ Potsdam ELF station catalog or a NOAA/USGS geomagnetic spectrum) that visually shows the inter-harmonic minimum. A figure or specific spectral plot citation would make the rebuttal one click away from a confirmation rather than requiring a paywalled book.

kernel_of_truth.description: Real MT spectra near 10–12 Hz have local complexity — cultural EM noise (50/60 Hz harmonics and sub-harmonics), site-dependent geological resonances, and the rising tail of the second Schumann mode. A specific MT survey under specific noise conditions can show a feature near 12 Hz that a reader untrained in spectral analysis could call a ‘peak.’ The dome is not inventing a region of the spectrum that has nothing in it (data/wins.json:WIN-009.detail_evidence cites the band as having structure, not silence).
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The claim is universality (‘consistent across independent datasets’), not site-specific feature. My quantitative spectrum check (Lens C below) gives relative power 0.263 at 12 Hz vs 1.055 at 7.83 Hz vs 0.637 at 14.3 Hz — 12 Hz is the slope between modes, not a local maximum. For a site-specific feature to elevate to a ‘universal telluric peak,’ the dome would need to (1) cite specific MT publications showing the peak across multiple sites and (2) provide a derivation from dome geometry (currently absent per data/wins.json:WIN-009.code_analysis.derives_from_dome=false). Neither is provided.

deeper_analysis: The c2 review concluded current_verdict_holds:true and recommended_action:no_change with three minor stylistic notes. None of those c2 notes have been addressed in current text — the only change since c2 is the post-hoc framework rollout that appended a standardized paragraph to detail_verdict_text (151 → 1114 chars). The verdict (Refuted by Data) and the core scientific argument (12 Hz is an inter-harmonic minimum; 14.3 Hz harmonic falsifies any cutoff) are unchanged and unaltered. So this c3 review focuses primarily on the new content and on the structural risks the c2 reviewer flagged but didn’t escalate. The two moderate findings above (data-fetch-line phrasing for hardcoded WINs; telluric-vs-Schumann distinction) are framework-level issues likely to recur on every post_hoc=true WIN, and they should probably be raised as cross-WIN issues to the decider rather than patched only on WIN-009.

citations_verified: Chave & Jones (2012), DOI 10.1017/CBO9781139020138 — Cambridge University Press, The Magnetotelluric Method: Theory and Practice. Same DOI used by WIN-008. Plausible per Cambridge’s CBO DOI prefix; this curmudgeon run did not have web access to re-resolve. Trusted from prior cycles.
https://john09289.github.io/predictions — dome home page URL, present in raw-text/01-home.txt and raw-text/02-wins.txt as the dome’s root.
Anchor #posthoc-standard — verified to exist as a <span id="posthoc-standard"> just before the p3-31b details element in data/sections.json:part3.html.
Anchor #win008 — standard build.js-emitted WIN panel id; verified consistent with cross-references used elsewhere in wins.json.
citations_unchecked: Specific MT survey datasets the dome implicitly relies on (‘Multiple independent MT literature sources’). Dome cites no DOIs or URLs, so there is nothing to verify.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A dome defender, accepting our methodological principles, argues: ‘Your refutation conflates atmospheric Schumann modes with telluric (subsurface) signals. WIN-009 is about magnetotelluric ground currents, where coupling, attenuation, and source distribution differ from the atmospheric ELF spectrum. Local MT surveys near tectonic boundaries do show elevated 10–13 Hz power that doesn’t map cleanly onto the spherical-cavity Lorentzian template you computed. The Schumann band is the source, but the surface telluric expression is filtered through Earth’s conductivity structure — and that filter can produce localized peaks that the cavity spectrum alone does not predict. Your review treats the two spectra as identical when in fact MT amplitude distributions are well-known to deviate from the canonical Schumann shape.’
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Add the bridge sentence proposed in hole #2 (‘MT signals at ELF frequencies are dominated by the same Earth–ionosphere cavity excitations measured atmospherically as Schumann modes…’) AND a second sentence acknowledging the defender’s real point: ‘Local conductivity structure can shape the telluric amplitude distribution, but it does not introduce a universal 12 Hz peak — it produces site-dependent variability in mode amplitudes, not a globally consistent inter-harmonic peak.’ Together these close the lane: yes, MT is filtered through the ground; no, the filter does not produce the universal feature the dome claims.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: All four related WINs treat the Schumann band as the same physical phenomenon: WIN-001 explains away Tesla’s 11.78 Hz as a transmitter-coherence number unrelated to Schumann modes; WIN-002/029 take the 7.83 Hz fundamental at face value as a cavity prediction; WIN-008 uses the 14.3 Hz first harmonic to falsify an 11.7 Hz ‘cutoff’; WIN-009 uses the same harmonic structure to falsify a 12 Hz ‘peak.’ No cross-WIN contradiction: in every case we treat measured Schumann modes as real and treat dome ‘peaks’ within the inter-harmonic minimum as misidentifications. The framework hangs together.

claims_checked: Inter-harmonic minimum is between 7.83 and 14.3 Hz (claimed in detail_evidence)
12 Hz has lower spectral power density than the Schumann peaks (claimed in detail_evidence)
First harmonic at 14.3 Hz is robustly observed (claimed in detail_evidence and tldr_verdict)
quantitative_verification.computations: Synthesized a 5-Lorentzian Schumann spectrum with peaks at (7.83, 14.3, 20.8, 27.3, 33.8) Hz, Q≈4.0–4.5, relative amplitudes (1.0, 0.55, 0.35, 0.22, 0.14) — standard observational template. Sampled 700–1500 in 0.01 Hz steps. Located global minimum between f1 and f2; checked spot values at 7.83, 11.7, 12.0, 14.3 Hz.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: Computed inter-harmonic minimum sits at ~10.79 Hz (relative power 0.21), not exactly at 12 Hz. Power at 12 Hz = 0.263; at 7.83 Hz = 1.055; at 14.3 Hz = 0.637. So 12 Hz is on the rising slope toward the first harmonic, with power roughly 25% of the fundamental and 41% of the first harmonic — well below either peak, confirming the ‘lower spectral power density than the Schumann peaks’ claim. The c2 ‘rising slope’ nuance was correct but never integrated into the prose; the current detail_evidence still says ‘between cavity harmonics’ which is right but slightly less precise than ‘on the rising slope between cavity harmonics, with no local maximum.’ No quantitative error found in our review; only an opportunity for tighter phrasing.
